history: In support of the University’s mission of promoting research studies and instruction in the natural and social sciences and the humanities, the Graduate School was organized during the school year 1966–1967 with an initial enrolment of 15 in the Master of Arts in Education Program. Dr. Cristian Alcantara was appointed the first Dean of the Graduate School. During the school year 1975–1976, the Doctor of Philosophy in Education major in Educational Management and the Master of Science in Mathematics programs were added to the offerings of the Graduate School. Dr. Fredeswinda Serrano, the former Vice President for Academic Affairs of Pampanga Agricultural College (PAC), was the first Ph.D. graduate of the University. After a year, the Graduate School started offering a Master’s in Business Administration.

From 1966 to the present, the Graduate School has offered the following degree programs: Ph.D. Educational Management, Ph.D. in Curriculum and Instruction, Ph.D. Public Administration, Ph.D. in Management, Doctor in Business Administration, Doctor of Public Health in Health Promotion and Education, Doctor of Information Technology, Master of Arts in Education, Master of Arts in Special Education, Master of Arts in Teaching, Master of Science in Mathematics, Master in Business Administration, Master in Public Administration, Master of Arts in Nursing, Master in Nursing, Master of Science in Information Technology, Master in Public Health, and Master of Science in Medical Laboratory Science among others.

The successful graduates of the Graduate School are regional directors of government agencies; school administrators, e.g., public school superintendents, supervisors, principals, and head teachers; government officials; businessmen, and military and police officers. It has likewise awarded honorary degrees to prominent educators, public officials, and well-known businessmen and dignitaries.

Having adopted the trimester scheme since the academic year 1983–1984, the Graduate School has been enrolling professionals including lawyers, medical doctors, engineers, accountants, executives in business and industry, public relations practitioners, and educational managers for professional growth.

In keeping faith with the University’s thrust of ensuring quality in its program offerings, the Graduate School embarked on its journey toward accreditation. On August 7–8, 2015, the Master of Arts in Education and Master in Business Administration were visited by PAASCU and were granted initial accreditation for three years, effective December 12, 2015. The same programs were visited by PAASCU on March 1–2, 2019, and were granted re-accreditation for five (5) years.

To ensure that its programs remain current and on par with the requirements set by globalization and internationalization of higher education and the new policies, standards, and guidelines of CHED, all curricula were revised and implemented effective AY 2021–2022.
Believing in the tenet that a school is only as strong as its faculty, the Graduate School continuously maintains a team of competent, experienced, and efficient faculty and administrators.

admissions.note: At least one Recommendation Form accomplished by a current or former professor or employer is required. The AUF Admissions Office sends the Recommendation Form link directly to your recommenders — enter their names and email addresses in your Personal Information Sheet when you apply through MyAU. Full details on the admissions process are published on the AUF website.

name: Integrated School
description: The Integrated School provides quality basic education from primary through senior high school, forming young Angeleneans with solid academic foundations and strong values.
vision: Born from the Heart of the Church, the Integrated School envisions to emerge as an exemplar school, promoting excellence in curriculum and instruction, community empowerment, and research and innovation for the protection and advancement of human dignity.
mission: Guided by the University mission of “total development of man for God and humanity,” the Integrated School is committed to providing quality basic education as a way of becoming an evangelized and evangelizing institution towards the holistic development of the child as an agent of societal transformation.
goals: Provide quality basic education program inspired by the teachings of Christ, geared towards excellence in the delivery of various learning areas, thus promoting cognitive, affective and psychomotor development for the good of humanity.

Promote research and innovation in curriculum and instruction through relevant and responsive programs in Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) and Humanities, fostering 21st century skills of communication and collaboration, critical thinking and problem-solving, coding and engineering skills and technology literacy, creativity, and lifelong learning skills.

Hone Catholicity and Christian values and ideals of students with peace education, global understanding, and character education through various curricular and co-curricular programs and activities, aligned with the goals of Christian Formation Education.

Develop responsible, law-abiding young leaders and citizens with deep love and concern for others, for the environment, the community and the country, manifested in their active involvement in community empowerment programs and advocacies.
Enhance the physical well-being, cultural orientation, and art appreciation of the students through various sports, recreational and cultural activities.

Produce graduates imbued with the characteristics of Christ-centeredness, social and environmental responsibility, wisdom and academic excellence, commitment to lifelong learning, creative, innovative and critical thinking, and peace and global understanding.

coreValues.intro: The AUF Integrated School is committed to the total development of the child based on Catholic Church teachings and Christian values. It nurtures its students to be “Mabuti, Magaling at May Malasakit sa Kapwa.”

items.name: Mabuti or Integrity of Character — “Virtus”
bullets: This core value implies courage, worth, integrity and character; to be virtuous means to be respectful, humble, kind, true and honest in all aspects of one’s life.

Inspired by the theological virtues of faith, hope and love, including the cardinal virtues of justice, prudence, temperance and fortitude, the Angelenean is expected to manifest integrity of character, shown as goodness in thought, speech and action.
Values faith, hope and love, and Christ-centeredness through respect, humility, kindness, truth and honesty in one’s thought, speech and action.
Manifests good manners and right conduct with deep respect and regard for others.
Abides by the rules and regulations of the school, the community and the country with respect for ethical standards and moral values.
Shows love and respect for God, humanity, the country and the environment.

items.name: Magaling or Competence for Excellence — “Veritas”
bullets: Commitment to learning and academic competence are, in essence, a search for that which is true; the school takes part in the ardent search for truth and its unselfish transmission to youth and to all those learning to think rigorously, so as to act rightly and to serve humanity better (Ex Corde Ecclesiae 2).
The Angelenean upholds the value of the search for truth and knowledge for brilliant disposition.
Demonstrates wisdom and competence for academic work, and commitment to lifelong learning.
Manifests pursuit of excellence and truth in speech and action.
Shows adeptness in time management, leadership responsibilities, and problem-solving activities for curricular or co-curricular activities.
Collaborates harmoniously with others as a competent member of a group.
Seeks help or lends a helping hand in times of challenges.
Demonstrates grit to accomplish academic tasks or life challenges.
Accomplishes remarkable output or valuable innovative or creative work.

items.name: May Malasakit sa Kapwa or Charity as Mission — “Caritas”
bullets: To practice care and concern for others is to be charitable of one’s self; charity is self-giving love, the highest form of love (John 15:13); to love is to offer one’s self for others. The Angelenean aspires to practice charity.
Practices concern and care for others, especially the poor, the disabled and the marginalized members of society through leadership, volunteerism, service, and acts of generosity.
Initiates or participates in school-based, classroom-based, and community-based advocacies for cultural understanding, peace, respect for human rights and preservation of the environment.
Contributes time and resources for the fulfillment of the mission of the school towards community empowerment, responsible citizenship, and societal transformation.

learningOutcomes.intro: The Angeles University Foundation envisions its graduates to be responsible and competent citizens of the country and the world, imbued with the core values of “Mabuti, Magaling at May Malasakit sa Kapwa.” The Integrated School envisions its graduates to demonstrate the following outcomes:

items.title: Christ-centeredness (Aligned with “Mabuti”)
bullets: Embracing Christ’s way of life through moral living and social accountability.
Adhering to truth and Christian values amidst challenges brought about by social, political, economic and environmental crises.
Practicing fairness, honesty and integrity in all aspects of life.
Demonstrating positive disposition, maturity and courage, rooted in Catholic values, in the face of adversity.

items.title: Social and environmental responsibility (Aligned with “Malasakit sa Kapwa”)
bullets: Fosters the ethical principle that an Angelenean has an obligation to act for the benefit of the community and the environment.
Engaging in advocacies for peace, cultural heritage and the environment.
Demonstrating concern through active involvement in socio-civic activities.
Practicing tolerance for differences in race, culture, and exceptionalities.
Respecting individual dignity and human diversity.

items.title: Wisdom and academic excellence (Aligned with “Magaling”)
bullets: Demonstrating competence in various disciplines and fields of study.
Collaborating skillfully and effectively with peers, colleagues, authorities and institutions.
Demonstrating proficiency in oral and written communication using various media.
Manifesting wise decision-making, judgment and evaluation relevant to specific disciplines and academic endeavors.

items.title: Commitment to lifelong learning (Aligned with “Magaling”)
bullets: Practicing the value of further development of skills and competence in their respective fields of endeavor.
Adapting to the demands of innovation in their fields of endeavor.
Manifesting growth in moral, spiritual, socio-civic, and physical aspects of their lives.
Participating in collaborative learning for continual improvement.

items.title: Creative, innovative and critical thinking (Aligned with “Magaling”)
bullets: Demonstrating skills and competence in producing creative or innovative work, using logical and critical thinking, scientific process, and competence with the use of technology.
Analyzing and evaluating arguments and evidence logically.
Generating ideas, producing novel and useful works, methods, and approaches to solve problems.
Demonstrating competence in scientific research process and the use of technology for innovation.

items.title: Peace and universal understanding (Aligned with “Malasakit sa Kapwa”)
bullets: Underscores the importance of wisdom and academic excellence towards promoting peace and universal understanding.
Possessing national and international perspectives towards peace and universal understanding.
Demonstrating awareness, knowledge and skills when interacting effectively in culturally or linguistically diverse contexts.
Advocating peace and cultural, historical, political, environmental, and technological understanding as means of responsible citizenship in the local, national, and international contexts.
Using academic competence and technological skills in promoting Christian ideals, nationalism, environmental protection, and global understanding.

studentOrganizations.name: Student Catholic Action Youth for Christ

activities.name: Community Development Services
activities.description: Engaging programs to support community service, social responsibility, and values-oriented outreach.

activities.name: Waste Management Program
activities.description: Promotes environmental awareness, sustainability, and proper waste segregation on campus.

activities.name: Drop Everything and Read (DEAR) Program
activities.description: A school-wide reading campaign designed to cultivate reading habits and literacy among students.

activities.name: Campus CHAMPS
activities.description: Comprehensive school activities promoting sportsmanship, character, and discipline among students.

activities.name: JEP (Just English Please) Campaign
activities.description: Encourages students to communicate in English to build language proficiency and global readiness.

activities.name: Item Analysis of Test
activities.description: Systematic evaluation of testing tools to ensure high standards of academic assessment.

activities.name: Leadership Training
activities.description: Seminars and workshops to develop character, accountability, and leadership potential in students.

activities.name: Intramurals
activities.description: Annual sports events promoting team spirit, physical wellness, and camaraderie.

activities.name: Quiz Bees
activities.description: Academic competitions across subjects to challenge and celebrate student intelligence.

activities.name: Science Camp
activities.description: Interactive science workshops, experimental learning, and stargazing sessions.

activities.name: Youth Camp
activities.description: Reflection, spiritual development, team-building, and values formation activities.

activities.name: Family Day Celebration
activities.description: A special day dedicated to parents and students celebrating unity and community.

activities.name: Parents' Encounter
activities.description: Special programs facilitating dialogue, connection, and joint growth of parents and school mentors.
